description: Dioscorea polystachya or Chinese yam, also called cinnamon-vine, is a species of flowering plant in the yam family. It is sometimes called Chinese potato or by its Korean name ma. It is also called huaishan in Mandarin and wàaih sāan in Cantonese.
origin: Central   S. China to Kuril Islands and Taiwan
synonyms: ["Dioscorea polystachya f. tsukune", "Dioscorea rosthornii", "Dioscorea polystachya f. elongata", "Dioscorea batatas f. flabellata", "Dioscorea doryphora", "Dioscorea potaninii", "Dioscorea batatas f. tsukune", "Dioscorea decaisneana", "Dioscorea batatas f. rakuda", "Dioscorea pseudobatatas"]

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day) | Climate: temperate
common_problems: No significant problems in this country.
However yams can be susceptible to fungal and viral diseases, scale, nematodes and mealybugs.
This species is listed as invasive by the NC Invasive Plant Council.
common_pests: However yams can be susceptible to fungal and viral diseases, scale, nematodes and mealybugs.
care.edible_parts: The tuber can be eaten raw or cooked but safest to cook the root

description: Dioscorea villosa is a species of twining tuberous vine which is native to eastern North America. It is commonly known as wild yam, colic root, rheumatism root, devil's bones, and fourleaf yam. It is common and widespread in a range stretching from Texas and Florida north to Minnesota, Ontario and Massachusetts.
origin: Canada, U.S.A.
synonyms: ["Merione villosa", "Dioscorea pruinosa", "Dioscorea longifolia", "Dioscorea lloydiana", "Dioscorea repanda", "Dioscorea villosa var. glabra", "Dioscorea villosa var. glabrifolia", "Dioscorea paniculata var. glabrifolia", "Dioscorea villosa var. hirticaulis", "Dioscorea villosa var. laeviuscula"]
